data.title: The Market Is Too Harsh On FactSet Research Systems
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Tomas Prosecky as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

The stock price of FactSet Research Systems Inc. (FDS) has been heavily punished in the last year, going down more than 50% from its all-time highs due to fears of AI competition. I believe these fears are both exaggerated and misguided, as what AI tools need the most is quality data, which FDS is ready to provide thanks to their investments into new products and partnerships. Furthermore, the company offers strong fundamentals and stability through its wide and ever-growing network of institutional clients and users. The company also offers a decent dividend yield of 2% and an active buyback program. Due to these reasons and a compelling valuation at roughly 15x P/E, I view FactSet Research Systems as a BUY at current prices.

Business and Strategy

FactSet Research Systems is an American technology company which offers financial data and software analytic tools made for investment professionals all around the world. Its main product is an integrated platform which helps institutional users in decision making, portfolio management, risk management and analysis. The company collects revenues mainly from subscription of this platform, which rivals platforms such as Bloomberg. The biggest added value of the company lies in their huge database of public and private data, and automation of analytical processes that comes with this data. Because of this, the company and its datasets are integrated around the entirety of the global financial world. Its clients are 3 of every 4 biggest global banking institutions or 95 of the 100 biggest asset management firms. This makes the product incredibly sticky, not just because financial professionals are used to it, but also because changes in these kinds of companies are typically slow due to high compliance standards. Furthermore, over 90% of current ASV (Annual Subscription Volume) is tied to proprietary advantages tied to specific client data or own proprietary data and tools, according to the management, based on the 2Q 2026 Presentation.

This information is in direct contrast to the price the market applies to FactSet’s stock at the moment. In the past year, the stock price has gone down over 47%, which is the highest decline among mid-cap data analytics companies such as S&P Global Inc. (SPGI), Moody’s Corporation (MCO) or Morningstar, Inc. (MORN). While the services of the first two mentioned are certainly stickier than FactSet’s due to their businesses of security ratings, I do believe that the market has turned too sour on FactSet Research, which itself provides high value, deeply embedded services to its clients that will be much harder to replace than first meets the eye.

FactSet has over 9 thousand total corporate clients and over 240 thousand individual users (managers, bankers and data analysts). The number of clients has risen by 9% in 2025, showing that the company still has room to grow. The retention rate of the Annual Subscription Volume, which serves pretty much as a proxy for the revenue in the upcoming 12 million, has historically always remained above 95%. The company also sometimes acquires smaller software firms for data analytics. An example is an acquisition of CUSIP from 2022, or LiquidityBooks in 2025. The company operates in three geographical segments (US, EMEA, Asia) and supports mainly 4 types of customers; Buy-side (49% of revenues), Dealmakers (19% of revenues), Wealth managers (16% of revenues) and Market Infrastructure (16% of revenues).

Why the Stock Is Down

There are three major reasons why the stock has performed so poorly over the last year, down 47% in the last year as of April 6th, 2026.

AI disruption: According to investors, AI tools can offer direct competition to FactSet services and make data accessibility easier.

Seat-based pricing model: Due to increased productivity from AI, financial institutions will employ fewer seat-based revenue in the future as their headcounts in analytical roles decrease.

Decreasing revenue growth: After the pandemic investment boom and CUSIP acquisition, revenue growth climbed up to 15% per year, the growth last year was just +6%.

I believe that the AI disruption risk is both exaggerated and misguided. AI, in the form of LLMs or Agents, cannot replicate the biggest added value of FactSet, that is a huge database of public and private data reaching back decades into the past. In fact, AI tools are notoriously imprecise with data collection and their ability to replace FactSet’s main use case I view as limited. While this advantage has been challenged in recent months by complex data collecting tools from either Claude or Perplexity, the easy reality is that there really is no reason for companies to move away from FactSet to these tools, as they are still unreliable (especially in data standardization), hard to maintain and generally much more costly than first meets the eye due to increasing price of token usage and storage costs. Furthermore, FactSet has spent decades collecting, cleaning and harmonizing data in direct communication with its clients. There is potential for AI tools to replace certain integrated processes FactSet offers, like tools used for equity analysis, market news or portfolio management, but FactSet itself has heavily expanded their portfolio of AI native tools which help automation processes for its users. Nowadays, it employs several AI products inside its software, such as integrated chatbots, automatic analysis of corporate results or conference calls, translation of reports or data analysis helpers.

Company news announced in recent months complete the picture that FDS is ready to work with AI, not go against it. In December 2025, they announced a new tool called MCP (Model Context Protocol) that enables LLMs and AI agents to integrate FactSet databases into their processes, giving the company another potential revenue stream, as data quality is what these models require the most. And I see the potential. During 2Q 2026 results it was announced that 48 out of FactSet’s 50 Top clients use at least 3 AI FactSet solutions, and the QoQ AI usage growth was +30%. Furthermore, the API volume usage of MCP itself grew 3x in between March and February with over 120 clients and many partnerships with platforms such as Amazon.com, Inc.'s (AMZN) AWS, Snowflake Inc. (SNOW), Claude or OpenAI (OPENAI). FactSet is investing heavily into data scaling and expansion of workflows that will make the AI experience even better for clients, but it also sees its own productivity gains from cloud optimization and AI deployment. With that said, the worker base of FactSet itself keeps on increasing (+1.9% yoy to over 12k workers), a trend I would like to see reverse in the upcoming years.

The second major reason why the stock has performed so poorly is not necessarily that the solutions of FactSet will be replaced, rather that increased productivity will lead to seat reduction in analytical roles of their clients. This would lead to direct hit into the revenue of FactSet, which is thought to be largely seat-based. Management realizes this and gives frequent updates on the seat-based exposure. In the 2Q 2026 call, they have said that direct seat-based exposure is now less than 20% of all ASV, and quickly coming down as the company actively works with its clients to move into usage-based pricing models. As they say, the more agents are used, the more high quality data they will need. Thus, they are optimizing for the future to not suffer from the difference in seat and usage-based pricing.

The third major reason why the stock has performed so poorly is the decrease of revenue growth since 2022. My explanation of this phenomenon is that the current revenue growth is the long-term norm and the high post-pandemic growth was mostly impacted by the huge equity investing boom and the acquisition of CUSIP. The slowdown of growth to current 5-7% YoY has mostly been a return to the long-term norm, as is visible from the graph below. The demand for FactSet products is still high, the number of clients rose by 9.5% in 2025 and the total value of subscription rose by 5.7%, but we should not expect this business to be a double-digit grower in the future. Not even adoption of data for AI usage can probably achieve that. The thesis is that FactSet is valued so poorly that even a long-term growth of 5-7% per year is enough to justify buying at current prices, thanks to the huge resilience of the business as well as good capital allocation.

FactSet in Numbers

FactSet is a company with a market cap of around $8.5 billion and EV currently around $9.7 billion. That’s for a company that generates $2.4 billion in annual sales which should consistently grow in mid to high single digits thanks to higher user penetration and price increases. Just recently FactSet has announced a big partnership with Barclays PLC (BCS) and several other business wins, as well as deeper integration of several of its new tools such as real time data feeds or MCP. GAAP operating margins have historically hovered around 30-32%, implying great power to generate profits from sales. While this is below industry peers like MSCI, S&P Global or Moody’s, it’s also higher than Morningstar, which I consider more of a close rival to FactSet specifically.

Now, one more implication of increased AI usage is that FactSet’s operating margins could come down as a result of higher investment costs, as we could see in 2025 and continue seeing in 2026, as despite revenue growing by 7.1% (as of the most recent 2Q report), operating income growth came in flat. Management has talked extensively about these investments and how critical they are to FactSet’s position on the market (as referenced in the 2Q 2026 Earnings Call), and I tend to agree. The important aspect is that they are not isolated investment, they are investments done in collaboration with huge international partners such as AWS, Claude, Snowflake or OpenAI. FactSet is becoming embedded into these platforms and it opens up new revenue streams and marketing potential. Management has explicitly said in the Q2 Call that Claude is the firms „fastest-growing marketing channel", implying that the LLM integration allows clients to consume more and more of their data. I believe that these strategic investments are aligned with the long-term strategy of the firm, which is to continue expanding their proprietary data moat and increase client integration through new products and services.

But FactSet is not just about investments. FactSet’s FCF margins have been very stable around 25%. Thus, from every $1 made on revenue, it generates roughly $0.25 for its shareholders. While management’s strategy relies heavily on re-investments into data infrastructure and occasional M&A, the payout dividend ratio is around 25-30% each year, which currently accounts for a near 2% dividend yield. Rest of FCF is used for opportunistic acquisitions or stock buybacks. The important fact about management quality is that as the stock price fell during 2025, so has buybacks increased. The current buyback yield is roughly 4% after excluding SBC, putting the overall shareholder awards at roughly 6%. As of the most recent 2Q 2026 Release, the company still had over $690 million left in its share repurchase program.

The last snapshot of how the company is doing came at the end of March, with the announcement of 2Q 2026 results. The company exceeded expectations on both top and bottom line and ended the quarter with a revenue growth of +7.1% and ASV growth of +6.7% YoY. While operating margins decreased by 220 bps yoy, this was expected and well communicated by the management. New client engagement, operational benefits and a robust demand (ASV retention rate above 95%) made the company update the FY26 guidance upwards. The company also bought back $163 million of shares in the quarter and has nearly $700 million left for further repurchases. Management underlined their long-term strategy for an AI driven future, trust in current investments as well as new partnerships.

Growth Drivers and Valuation

The main question surrounding FDS is whether and how fast can the company continue growing its top line. The historic revenue growth has hovered around 5-7% p.a., under normal circumstances, and I believe that this level can continue into the future. This is due to a combination of new partnerships (LLM and Cloud providers), big contract wins (Barclays), expansion of task-based and API revenues, as well as frequent price increases of current products. Management had said that clients are accepting their price increases well due to the value and workflow integration their tools are providing, and the strong ASV retention rate above 95% is a strong indicator of that. If we assume that the company can realize a price increase of 2% each year, it would only need to grow its volume of sales by 3.5% to achieve a growth rate of 5.5%, which is the rate I use in my valuation approach. This volume growth rate is much below its long-term client and user growth rate (that is closer to +8% per year), and while AI implementation could present some seat-based pressure, the company is investing further into usage-based and API revenue streams through new analytic and real time data tools.

With these assumptions in mind, I have constructed 3 multiple-based valuation scenarios for the intrinsic valuation of FactSet Research. In the Base case, I expect revenues to grow by the aforementioned +5.5% for the next 3 years with operating margins of 30%, both of which are well within management expectations for 2026. In the Bear case, I imply that the revenue growth will slow down to +3% CAGR due to AI pressure and operating margins will fall to 28% due to further investments into new products and worse product mix. In the Bull case, I present a world where the revenue growth picks up steam following AI partnerships to +8% CAGR, and operating margins increase to 32% thanks to operational efficiencies. The stability of FactSet's business and the long-term nature of their contracts doesn't provide much need for wider revenue growth or margin estimates. In all cases I count with a conservative 2% net buyback yield (after Stock-Based Compensation), a stable dividend of $4.40 per year (2% yield) and a tax rate of 19% as per management expectations for 2026. For the exit multiple, I select 15x P/E for the Bear case, 20x P/E for the Base case and 25x P/E for the Bull case, all below historic averages the company used to have.

The point of these three approaches and the selected P/E multiples is that even if FactSet's performance slows down massively, as shown in the Bear case, the valuation still doesn't look completely off. Considering that the Bear case itself projects a negative scenario that is much below the expectations of both management and Wall Street analysts, I deem this highly unlikely. If the Base scenario is met, which I constructed to be slightly below management estimates to remain conservative, it implies a yield of 19.43% p.a. over the next 3 years, including dividends. If we were to apply this growth linearly, the target price for the next 12 million and 36 million would be $276.37 and $381.04, respectively. That seems a highly compelling opportunity for such a stable and mature company.

Thus, while the market remains fixated on the potential risks of AI disruption, I believe that several near-term and mid-term catalysts could prove the resiliency of the business and drive the stock price toward my Base and Bull case targets:

Monetization of new tools

Rising adoption and growing revenue of new products, such as real-time feeds or MCP, could prove to the market that FactSet has a place in the AI-driven world of the future.

Growing user base and price increases

The company keeps winning big corporate deals that can drive its user-base higher. Furthermore, they are successful in raising prices of their products.

Transition to usage-based pricing

Continued transition of direct seat-based revenue to usage-based models could make the risk of headcount reduction much smaller.

Shareholder value

The company pays out a 2% dividend yield and has nearly $700M remaining in their share repurchase plan.

Operating margin stabilization

The market is punishing FactSet for flat operating income growth as the company invests heavily into new products. This does not mean the company’s operating leverage is falling, it means the company is securing its long-term standing position on the market.

Risks

But no investment is without its risks. Though the case for AI growth seems compelling in my eyes, the investment thesis could always turn sour if FactSet in fact starts losing corporate clients or if seat-based revenue starts decreasing heavily. As it stands, seat-based revenue is still directly responsible for 20% of all revenues, and indirectly responsible for probably much more, so a great reduction in seats could prove problematic for my thesis. This risk grows in significance if recession hits the global economy, which, with the recent development in Middle East, grows ever more likely. The company also employs a significant employee force, over 15% higher than Morningstar does on the same market valuation, and only 4x lesser than that of S&P Global’s, while the rating giant is more than 15x larger in market capitalization. Effectiveness in this area should be achieved in order to truly unlock the operating leverage the company has.

One could also say that I am underestimating the power of AI capabilities in the future. After all, Claude seems to be releasing new functionalities every other day, won’t AI just do everything by itself, including data collection? It’s certainly possible, which is why I am not all-in on the stock and I still recommend to diversify accordingly. But the simple answer is that I believe it won’t, and even if it does, it will still need a quality source of data to perform. My arguments for this future are scattered throughout this review, but let me add one bit: Claude’s announcements are a great marketing tool. Every week a new feature is announced, and every week that feature starts a discussion about how many jobs will be obsolete in 6 months time. The reality of that is that people were already making the same claims 6 months back, and very little has changed in the world of professional finance. AI is revolutionary, I am not discussing anything on that front, but it will take time, and it will be much more complicated than just writing a prompt into Claude and expecting to replace half of your software base. The truth is that the AI-usage bubble has convinced investors that everything will change in a click, while real life corporations are slow and riddled with compliance and bureaucracy. Before they make a move, FactSet will have its own perfectly usable AI solutions (in fact, it already has), and the companies wanting to implement these features will come to them, a reliable long-term client in the space.

But of course, in the short-term, the stocks can still achieve strong volatility, as the software apocalypse seems to still be ongoing.

Summary and Reasons to Buy

To summarize, I really like FactSet stock at its current valuation due to multiple reasons:

Data moat

The company's competitive advantage lies in its massive database of public and proprietary data, gathered throughout its network of partnerships, acquisitions and decade long research.

Integration in the financial world

FactSet services are standardly used in most global banks and by 95 of the Top 100 largest asset managers in the world. Contracts with firms are long-term, typically lasting 5 to 7 years.

AI as a growth factor

The company itself is investing in AI analytical tools, integrating language models into its software solutions, and has recently started providing its databases directly to language models through MCP. The company is therefore not afraid of AI, but actively embraces this trend.

But most importantly, FactSet shares are currently trading at a significant historical discount (15x P/E vs. a historical average of around 25-30x), yet in my opinion, the long-term investment thesis remains fully intact. While I don’t necessarily expect the company to go back up to these historical multiples, the investment thesis does not need that, as demonstrated in my valuation model. If the future growth rate remains at current levels, something I strongly believe in, the stock should generate sufficient alpha. Furthermore, the company pays a stable dividend of 2% p.a. and announced a plan to repurchase up to $1B in shares from the market in the coming years, with $700M from that plan still remaining to be deployed.

I believe that long-term believers in quality data will be rewarded.

data.title: SHYG: Prices Front-Of-Mind For Embattled Consumers, Risk For Consumer Cyclical Credit
data.description:
The iShares 0-5 Year High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (SHYG) was looking up in our previous coverage as the inflation battle seemed to have been won in the US. That no longer seems to be the case, with the Iran War having failed to be resolved through diplomatic means once again, and therefore there is YTM upside in our view through both of the two macro components of a SHYG bet:

  • An important credit spread component, with the credit rating of SHYG bonds being quite poor modally at B and BB.
  • And in interaction with the credit spread component, a general sensitivity to all YTM shifts, whether from credit spreads or from changes in the yield curve, due to a duration of 2.04 years.

Last week saw a return of risk-on feeling as credit spreads fell and the markets saw somewhat of a recovery. We think this is premature. Firstly, there are actual signs of reinflation already, and this poses a risk to the broader economy and also is upside on YTMs, which matters for all FI bets with duration. Secondly, it was recently announced that ceasefire talks with Iran have failed, meaning higher conflict risks, more transit risks for vessels, and escalation risks, as the US has announced its Navy will blockade the Strait and prevent logistics with Iranian ports - friendlier ports will be unmolested. Credit spreads might rise as much as 0.5%, and there is probably some yield curve upside too. Since SHYG has some duration, it's placed unfavorably in terms of possible price action. We don't think it's a better place to park than cash right now.

SHYG Breakdown

SHYG is high yield, and that reflects also in some of the sector composition. There is a lot of consumer cyclicality with discretionary end markets and less constant demand. Comms are also featured highly, which are capital-intensive and often carry more debt.

The benchmark index is of liquid high-yield bonds with maturity less than 5 years; therefore, there is still some duration, which is effectively at 2 years.

The high-yield credit spreads are shown above. Note that they are far below the Ukraine war outbreak levels, which was our last energy crisis, and they have also come down significantly in the last week, reflecting that risk-on feeling that came into markets.

The yield curve hasn't moved much since the beginning of the Iran war but has moved up significantly from previous levels, reflecting a broad-based reinflationary assumption for markets.

Bear Case

  • It may not be sustainable for credit spreads to be so low. In addition to being lower than in 2022, which was a less serious energy crisis where assets weren't stranded and there was recourse (indeed, Russian supply finds its way without issues even after sanctions into some part of the global market), they have come down in the last week, where it's become clear with the failure of the ceasefire talks that the war is not coming to an end and all parties seem willing to continue the fighting, with the Iranian regime likely undeterred by strikes that would only harm citizens such as those threatened last week on power infrastructure, and the US and allies clearly incentivized to reopen the Strait, Iran's main bargaining chip. High oil prices are likely to continue in the near term. Moreover, the data that shows falling consumer sentiment precisely on price concerns in the US is a risk for the SHYG's heavy consumer discretionary lean. Credit spreads are likely to come up in the near term, perhaps as much as 0.5% to the recent local highs or perhaps more than 2.5% to the Ukraine war outbreak highs. FI is supposed to be relatively safe, so these movements interacting with a 2-year duration are a needless standing in the way of capital risk.
  • For benchmark rates and the yield curve, we don't see things coming down as the price at the pump has grown in the 20% area, consumer inflation expectations are coming up, and the fact that it's affecting consumer sentiment means it could come up even more. It may give energy to a wage-price spiral that would persist inflation and prevent the shock from dissipating.

Bull Case

There is also a bull case:

  • The blockade is on Iranian ports, and with the active presence of the US Navy in de-mining and also in deterring attacks that would invite significant retaliation onto Iran, it is possible that transit to and from friendlier Gulf ports could resume and therefore supply could improve.
  • The blockade could also be successful in that it pressures the Iranians into accepting the terms laid forth in Islamabad this weekend, and perhaps the war ends. This is probably wishful thinking.
  • This would reduce the risk of higher baseline inflation rates by breaking the back of inflation sooner rather than later, and also it would likely lend some support to consumers who are worried about pricing risks and justify some of the downward movements that have already occurred in the credit spreads. Therefore, YTMs might fall a bit more or at least be justified in having fallen so far.

Bottom Line

At a 2-year duration, exposure to a 0.5% or 1% increase in YTMs means a 1-2% hit, as duration is a scalar of YTM changes to describe price risk for the bonds, in this case 2 years or a 2x scalar. Back to Ukraine war levels would be a 3% or perhaps a 6% price hit mostly contributed to by higher credit spreads, as the yield curve has stayed quite high, not reflecting recent short-term ebullience around the possibility of the ceasefire talks, which have not succeeded. 6% is more significant and is the sort of drawdown even equity portfolios could expect in a turbulent environment.

We cannot predict if the blockade will work; it could go either way, though we speculate that the Iranian regime has nothing to lose and will keep fighting as long as possible. But we'd rather play cheaper equities that don't have optimistic credit spread assumptions baked into them rather than SHYG, which will likely experience disappointment over the ceasefire talks at the opening, and it may dawn on markets that a diplomatic solution might not be coming at all unless the blockades have an effect in bringing the end of the war. The fact that credit spreads have come down before a solid solution to end the war has been presented makes us uninterested in moving from cash into SHYG, where a credit-based rally is premature in our view. Moreover, with consumer sentiment still falling, showing how front of mind these price considerations are, we are worried about consumer inflation expectations, which are surely creeping up again and re-igniting too much inflation again. SHYG isn't compelling.

We do want to point out positively that SHYG does have a lower expense ratio than alternatives at 0.3%, where comps are at 0.4%, like the State Street SPDR Bloomberg Shrt Trm Hg Yld Bd ETF (SJNK), or higher, like the PIMCO 0-5 Year High Yield Corporate Bond Index ETF (HYS). They are very similar ETFs also in terms of performance.

data.title: FBDC: Actively Managed BDC Exposure
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

It has been a while since we've touched on the FT Confluence BDC & Specialty Finance Income ETF (FBDC); in fact, this was the First Trust Specialty Finance and Financial Opportunities Fund under the "FGB" ticker previously. At that time, they were looking to merge the fund into the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund (AOD). However, they were unsuccessful in getting the votes required for approval. Thus, they ended up reorganizing the fund from a closed-end fund structure to an ETF. Shortly after that time, they also conducted a 1-for-5 reverse split.

Since that all took place, business development companies ("BDCs") had been under some significant pressure. That has seen FBDC head on a pretty sharp and steady decline. While FBDC has a flexible investing policy that can incorporate investments outside of BDCs, the fund is and has historically been almost all BDC-focused. On occasion, we'd sometimes see mREIT exposure and even Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), but it had often been 90%+ BDCs. The new ticker of "FBDC" for this fund makes me assume that they will be even more focused on BDCs.

FBDC Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 12.22% SEC yield, 12.37% Distribution Yield
  • Expense Ratio: 0.95% (total expense 13.69% with AFFE)
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $33.332 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

FBDC's investment objective is "a high level of current income. As a secondary objective, the Fund seeks an attractive total return." In an effort to achieve that, the fund will "invest at least 80% of its net assets in a portfolio of securities of business development companies and other speciality finance companies that the Confluence Investment Management LLC, the Fund's investment sub-advisor, believes offer attractive opportunitfor income and capital appreciation."

Performance Comparison

FBDC is a fairly small fund, and that likely is why it tends to get overlooked by most investors. Given that the restructuring took place at the end of June 2025, we don't have too much history in this wrapper yet. That said, given the fund's primary exposure, comparing it to VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) can make sense. That is a passively managed ETF that focuses on replicating the MVIS U.S. Business Development Companies Index. There is also the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC).

Similar to FBDC, PBDC is another actively managed ETF. That means the fund managers aren't necessarily looking to replicate any specific index returns (minus fees). Therefore, they have the flexibility to invest in where and what BDCs they would like. The end goal would be to obviously attempt to outperform something like BIZD.

Given a look back at the relatively short track record so far, it has been FBDC that has taken the crown.

I mean...a crown not necessarily worth boasting over, as it is the 'crown of not being as bad' during this period. This is also only historical performance, and that means no guarantee of future results.

If we look back further historically, the annualized performance is provided when the fund was FGB by the fund sponsor. That can help to provide an even longer track record with several caveats.

In the table, they also provide the comparison against the MarketVector U.S. Business Development Companies Index. In this case, the fund has actually done fairly poorly. Which I believe is worth pointing out, since the short-term track record since the conversion has been better.

This could be the result of now focusing more specifically on BDCs over other financial investments—therefore, better overall active management. FGB was also a leveraged fund, and FBDC has decided to avoid adding leverage to already heavily leveraged instruments.

Still, if picking between FBDC and alternatives, investors may tend to avoid FBDC because of its only around $33 million in AUM. That can be compared to PBDC's $253.3 million and BIZD's relatively massive $1.46 billion AUM. That can give some investors pause as it results in lower relative daily trading volume.

With that said, remember that in an ETF wrapper, the liquidity is actually driven primarily by the underlying holdings and how liquid they are. ETFs have a creation/redemption mechanism, which helps to ensure that the share price trades tight to the NAV per share. However, it also means that if there is a large increase in demand, new units can be created by authorized participants.

Another hesitation for investing in FBDC or the others is what looks like a massive expense ratio. To be fair, it does come with massive expenses because of the underlying BDCs, not the actual ETF structure. In the case of FBDC, the management fee comes in at 0.95%. Pushing it to the 13.69% total expense ratio is because of the underlying BDC's own expenses, which get reflected in the ETF breakdown of fees.

This does make FBDC the more expense fund, with BIZD's total expense ratio at 12.86% and slightly higher than PBDC's 13.49%. Given that BIZD is a passive index-tracking ETF, the management fee comes in at 0.40%. PBDC's management fee is 0.75%. That can make the FBDC outperformance in this period slightly even more impressive, as they had to overcome the slightly higher management fee.

In comparison, though, the underlying acquired fund fees and expenses are all relatively similar and are by far the largest contributing factor to expenses. For that reason, looking forward, the edge could only be given slightly to PBDC and BIZD.

FBDC pays out a monthly distribution while PBDC and BIZD pay quarterly. There are a lot of BDCs that pay monthly now, so going the quarterly route doesn't seem to make a lot of sense, at least in my opinion. It is also generally more appealing to have the monthly payout for income-focused investors.

Another thing that FBDC has kept from a more traditional CEF structure is the level payout as well. That is, they tend to keep the same payout for several months or even years at a time. The latest here has been $0.175 (what looks like a large payout was $0.8750, but adjusted for the split was the same $0.175).

BIZD and PBDC both pay out variable rates based on their own underlying dividends received. Either payout schedule has its own pros and cons.

With a level payout, investors tend to know what they will be paid for an extended period of time. That can make what an income investor is going to receive fairly predictable. Of course, there is always the ability to make adjustments, and that can be after months or even years. When those adjustments take place, which can result in lower payouts, investors can often get frustrated.

This is very relevant for this fund currently. With FBDC's dividend yield now pushing 12.37%—I'd have to suggest that a cut could be healthy and is probably in the cards. At this point, with the SEC Yield coming in at 10.46%, it highlights that the fund is very likely overpaying. This is on the back of several BDCs reducing their own dividends and/or reducing the specials they had been paying.

That's where the variable rate can be beneficial; there really is no surprise in the next announcement of the payout because investors already know it will be changing based on income received. They also won't be overpaying the distribution to investors, because it'll be based on actual underlying earnings.

FBDC's Portfolio

In the BDC space, overall, looking forward, the outlook is a bit blurry. We've seen a number of private credit issues pop up that have sent jitters throughout the BDC space. Thus, the situation we are in currently is where BDCs have been under significant pressure. We've seen a lot of BDCs report their NAVs heading lower while non-accruals tick higher. They were also facing earnings pressure as the Fed was reducing its benchmark rate, putting pressure on the primarily floating-rate loans they invest in.

That said, there were some outliers that were able to hold NAV flat or see small increases. That's where actively managed funds can get a bit of an edge, as they can concentrate more fully on BDCs that tend to perform well. They aren't stuck investing like BIZD in names simply because of how large they are in terms of AUM.

For FBDC, the largest holdings are some of the best names that you can find. That includes Main Street Capital (MAIN)—always expensive but always performing well, with its massive premium that facilitates share issuances. Those share issuances then contributing positive to NAV accretion, helping to provide overall support of a rising NAV.

There is also Ares Capital Corp (ARCC), an externally managed BDC, but one that has probably the most stable long-term track record, despite the external structure. Then there is also Hercules Capital (HTGC), another name that is internally managed and has historically provided solid results.

Conclusion

FBDC was formerly FGB, a closed-end fund that emphasized a focus on BDC exposure. Since its reorganization last year, they have become even more dominated by BDC exposure now. Relative to the other BDC-focused alternatives, it has been able to see lower declines. That said, it has still been fairly sharp declines. Going forward, I think that BDCs are starting to look more fairly tempting. Investing in something like FBDC could be a decent alternative to gain significant diversification in the BDC in one wrapper. For investors who choose to invest and manage their own BDC holdings, perhaps they can get some ideas from some of the top names held in FBDC.

data.title: Euronet Worldwide: Time To Pay Up For The Payment Provider
data.description:
Shares of Euronet Worldwide (EEFT) have been struggling recently, with investors fearing the soft positioning of the firm catching up with the business and its shares down the road. Share prices have been falling, and while organic growth is not convincing, earnings growth continues, and great earnings yields are seen here.

Trading at just about 7 times realistic forward earnings, positioning concerns remain. At some point a combination of a great earnings yield, as well as stable revenues and still-growing earnings per share, makes the shares cheap enough, although the positioning concerns make it difficult to have conviction in size here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Non-Inspiring Performance

In February, Euronet reported a 6% increase in 2025 sales to $4.24 billion, albeit that revenues were up 4% on a constant currency basis. While reported revenue growth came in at 6% in the final quarter of the year, organic revenue growth slowed down to just a percent.

GAAP operating profits rose by 5% to $530 million, yet in between higher taxes and some other items, net earnings rose by just a percent to $313 million. Thanks to a mid-single-digit decline in the share count, earnings did advance by nearly forty cents to $6.84 per share.

Adjusted earnings rose by a dollar to $9.61 per share, and while some adjustments seem fair, that does not apply to a $55 million stock-based compensation expense, which after taxes works out to around a dollar per share.

The slower pace of growth towards year-end is attributed to economic stress among low-income households, as well as immigration policy uncertainty. Despite these headwinds, the company sees adjusted earnings growth in the range between 10% and 15% in 2026. So far, part of this growth is supported by continued efforts to grow the business. The company started the year 2026 with an agreement to acquire CrediaBank's merchant acquiring business, the 5th largest bank in Greece, although this reads as a smaller bolt-on deal.

Tough Times

Back in August I concluded that Euronet was paying the price of its positioning, with investors questioning the sustainability of key business segments. Beyond a huge ATM network, covering more than 50,000 locations, Euronet furthermore offers electronic payment processing solutions, as well as retail payment solutions and money transfer.

In fact, money transfer comprises about 40% of revenues, this being a solidly profitable business that posts margins in the lower teens. The ETF business, which includes the ATM networks, makes up nearly 30% of sales, these being very lucrative activities with margins reported around 20% of sales. Epay makes up for the remainder of sales, with margin reported in the low teens.

While the organic performance of the business and positioning can be questioned, the reality is that a $1 billion business in the Great Financial Crisis in 2008 doubled to $2 billion in 2014. Since then, sales have convincingly broken the $4 billion mark, all while continued net buybacks have been delivered upon. In fact, shares peaked around the $170 mark around the time of the pandemic.

Trading around the $100 mark last summer and with realistic earnings seen around $7.50 per share, equity was valued at around 13 times earnings. Net debt was reported at 1.1 times, that is, if we exclude cash holdings in ATMs. This net debt load would be on the increase after a near quarter of a billion dollar acquisition of CoreCard Corp., adding innovative credit technology solutions and processing business.

All in all, leverage was manageable and earnings multiples were very low, while growth was delivered upon, yet the positioning was very concerning. This made it appealing to like the valuation, but I lacked the triggers and feared disruption too much to get involved. Net debt was reported at just shy of a billion if we exclude cash held in ATMs, for a leverage ratio in the low 1s.

What Now?

Based on adjusted earnings around $8.50 per share reported for 2025 and the company guiding for these earnings to basically improve a long way to $9.50-$10.00 per share this year, valuation multiples have come in a great deal. Trading at $68, shares are down to levels seen in 2015, making that trade at just 8 times realistic earnings and a lower multiple based on the forward growth guidance, with shares trading around 7 times forward earnings.

As a testament to the valuation multiples having compressed, Euronet has more than doubled sales compared to 2015, when revenues just trailed $2 billion, all while margins have been rather flat and the share count has been down by a fifth. This really shows the degree of valuation multiple deflation taking place here.

The company actually commented on the concern about relevance, claiming that resilience is in its DNA, with the digital transformation being real and the company having a growth mindset. The company has successfully navigated the Great Financial Crisis, COVID-19, the turmoil in Greece, and others.

The reality is that positioning concerns remain with me, despite reassuring words issued by management; yet, on the other hand, the business enjoys quite solid diversification between countries and segments/technologies. While organic growth is not too impressive, the reality is that a 13 times realistic earnings multiple has collapsed to just 7 times in between a big setback in the share price and, moreover, continued earnings growth.

While leverage has picked up a bit in between M&A efforts and buybacks, at some point the earnings yield is convincing enough to see appeal amidst dirt-cheap valuations. Even as I cannot identify the great trigger here, a continuation of earnings per share growth and near mid-double-digit earnings yield looks convincing enough to start buying into the shares. The positioning concerns, however, make it so that I fail to have the conviction to get involved in size.

data.title: Knife River Remains Compelling As Focus On Growth Achieves Solid Growth
data.description:
Over the last several months now, the stock market has not really been in a great place. The S&P 500 is down 1.7% since I last wrote about Knife River Corporation (KNF) in early October 2025. By comparison, however, this aggregate play is up 3.6%. Considering that I called it a "Buy" back then, I can only say that I am happy about the outcome. Financial performance for the business has been robust, and management anticipates continued growth this year, with a lot of that almost certainly coming from acquisition activities. Although the stock might not be as cheap as some investors would like it to be, it is still attractive on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. This justifies, in my view, maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate right now.

Knife River is a solid play

Perhaps the best place to start with when it comes to analyzing Knife River Corporation would be the most recent data available. This is data covering the last quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that quarter, revenue amounted to $755.1 million. That was up 14.9% over the $657.2 million that the business reported a year earlier. As revenue increased, profitability also rose. Net income jumped from $23.3 million to $32 million. But that's not all. We saw operating cash flow expand from $172.4 million to $195.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a more than doubling from $45.1 million to $103.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $78.8 million to $116.8 million.

The chart above shows financial results for 2025 as a whole compared to 2024. Revenue increased, but profitability for the year as a whole ended up being mixed. This is disappointing to say the least. But what matters most here is that the company is showing some signs of strong performance. The final quarter of 2025 was particularly telling, and that strength occurred across both of its operating categories.

The largest of these right now happens to be the Construction Materials space. The final quarter saw revenue come in at $407.9 million. That was up nicely from the $355.8 million reported a year earlier. The company benefited during this time from a rise in volumes and, when it came to aggregates and ready-mixed concrete, higher prices. Aggregate volumes jumped 17% year over year for the final quarter, while ready-mix concrete volumes increased 20%. The price per ton of aggregates rose from $17.14 to $18.53, while the price per cubic yard for ready-mix concrete increased from $195.57 to $204.09. We did also see a rise in asphalt volumes totaling 8.7%, but the price per ton actually contracted from $71.32 to $67.79.

Management continues to benefit from a combination of price increases and acquisition activities. For 2025 as a whole, the firm enjoyed a mid-single-digit increase in pricing thanks to robust demand. However, we should not ignore the role that acquisition activities played in the health of the business. For context, the company spent just $348 million on capital expenditures last year. But it spent about $610 million on acquisitions. The largest of these, by far, was its purchase in March of 2025 of Strata Corporation, a construction materials and contracting services provider in North Dakota and northwestern Minnesota that offers 30 years of aggregate reserves, 24 ready-mix plants, three asphalt plants, a construction unit, and a portfolio of rail and trucking assets. This cost shareholders $454 million. But clearly, it is working out nicely. This also proved instrumental in pushing the backlog for the company’s Contracting Services segment up from $745.6 million to $1.03 billion.

Speaking of the Contracting Services, for the final quarter, revenue of $347.2 million comfortably beat out the $301.4 million reported a year earlier. About 81% of the revenue generated by this segment came from the public sector, with 65% involving streets and highways. But we also had airports, bridges, marine infrastructure, and more, and all sought out the company for its services. The remaining 19% of revenue came from private projects like buildings and site work, residential units, streets and highways, and more.

Something that should help not only this side of the business but also the Construction Materials side of the business is the fact that significant infrastructure spending will be needed over the next several years. Management estimated that, between 2024 and 2033, public infrastructure spending required just to maintain current needs will be about $2.2 trillion. 22% of the IIJA funds allocated by the federal government have yet to be committed within the areas in which Knife River Corporation operates. And 46% of those funds have yet to be distributed. Department of Transportation budgets are also on the rise. Spending in 2025 amounted to $61 billion for the states that Knife River Corporation operates in. But that number should grow to $69 billion this year. And then we also have the fact that while private construction might be down right now, in the long run, it should continue growing. Management estimates that, between 2025 and 2030, non-residential spending will expand from $1.19 trillion to $1.40 trillion. That's an increase of about 18%.

It's also worth noting that the company operates in some of the fastest-growing states in the nation. From 2020 through 2030, it was estimated that the population growth in the states in which it operates would be about 7%. This compares to 5% for states that it does not operate in and 6% for the United States as a whole. Management also provided data for the 2020 through 2040 window, as well as for the 2020 through 2050 window. As the image below illustrates, we are looking at substantial growth relative to what states in which it does not operate should achieve.

To capitalize on these opportunities, management is continuing to make acquisitions. In February, for instance, the company announced the purchase of Morgan Asphalt, which operates as an aggregate-spaced asphalt paving company based out of Utah. We don't know specifics about it, but with 250 people employed during peak construction season, it is certainly a serious enterprise. And then, more recently, in March of this year, management announced the acquisition of assets owned by Donaldson Brothers Ready Mix, which is an aggregates-based ready-mix supplier in western Montana. This actually makes the third acquisition that the company has made in its Mountain segment this year alone.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management expects revenue of between $3.30 billion and $3.50 billion. Even at the low end, that's above the $3.15 billion that the company reported for last year. From this, we should get EBITDA of between $520 million and $560 million. This compares favorably to the $484.3 million that the company reported for last year. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise similarly, we would get net profits of $175.2 million and adjusted operating cash flow of about $411.8 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the results achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are priced at the moment. These are actually pretty attractive multiples if you ask me. And compared to other similar enterprises, the stock is trading a bit on the cheap end as well. In that chart, you can see that only one of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. And only two of them are cheaper when it comes to both the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach and the EV-to-EBITDA approach.

Takeaway

The way I see it, Knife River Corporation looks to be an appealing prospect at this time. The company is attractively priced and growing nicely. I do worry about the state of the economy more broadly. But on the whole, I don't see a lot of long-term risk here. We might see some short-term disruptions, though given how the stock is priced and the track record that management has of consistent growth, I would view any sort of weakness as a bump in the road rather than a long-term issue. This is especially the case when you consider how much of what the company does is tied to public funding, not only at the federal level but at the state level as well. Because of this, I firmly believe that maintaining it as a "Buy" makes sense here.

data.title: 1999 Vs. 2026: No Contest
data.description:
The AI Revolution has increasingly been compared to the last major technology paradigm shift in the U.S., largely known as the Internet Boom. And it is a good comparison on many fronts. The birth of the internet ushered in greater U.S. productivity. The huge rally in the markets from 1995 to 1999 was largely driven by the major firms driving that revolution, like Cisco Systems (CSCO), much like the rally from 2023 to 2025 was spearheaded by those on the vanguard of the AI revolution, like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

Both the spikes in stocks driven by the deployment of the Internet and the AI Revolution left the overall market trading at extreme measures viewed via myriad traditional valuation metrics. Both technology paradigm shifts drove a huge surge in tech spending that substantially boosted GDP growth for years. However, that is where the comparison largely ends, and viewed dispassionately, 1999 versus 2026 ends with the conclusion that the period of the birth of the internet was significantly superior to the time we are living in now, both from an economic and market perspective.

Demographics:

Back in 1999, the Baby Boomers were in their prime earning years, and the median age of the U.S. was over four years younger with higher fertility rates as well. This led to a labor participation rate of more than 500 bps higher than where it is now.

More individuals in the workforce resulted in a much more favorable budget picture as well. More individuals were paying payroll and other taxes, and fewer were drawing from Social Security, Medicare, and other costly government programs. The U.S. ran a small fiscal surplus in 1999, compared to the roughly $2 trillion annual fiscal deficits in recent years. The total federal debt was less than $6 trillion as the 20th century came to a close. It is rapidly approaching $40 trillion now.

Geopolitical Factors:

In 1999, the U.S. sat atop the unipolar world, following the end of the Cold War. Russia was in disarray, and China was largely mired in poverty and had not been welcomed into the global trading community via the WTO. The U.S. had a 'peace dividend' to bank, and there were no major conflicts the country was involved in.

Compare that to the situation today. The U.S. has been heavily involved in a proxy war in Ukraine for over four years. Yet another conflict in the Middle East has been entered into. This has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a major global chokepoint, since the end of February. This has resulted in a huge spike in energy, fertilizer, and other key commodity prices. The administration is now planning to request $1.5 trillion for defense in FY2027, even as the country is already mired in debt. China has rapidly evolved to be the top economic and military rival to the United States over the past quarter century.

Infrastructure Buildout:

Bringing the internet to the masses was not cheap. In the five years following the groundbreaking Telecommunications Act of 1996, telecommunication equipment providers would spend a cumulative $500 billion putting in place the network that would enable this new technology. This effort largely involved laying fiber optic cable, adding new switches, and building wireless networks.

A large sum of money, to be sure. But this pales in comparison to the over $650 billion the four hyperscalers alone plan to spend in FY2026 to build out a network of multi-billion-dollar AI data centers and associated AI infrastructure and components like GPUs.

Nor did the buildout of the internet network require a huge capacity increase to the electrical grid to support this technology. This is rapidly becoming a huge constraint on the projected data center buildout. Some recent articles on Bloomberg and other sites have projected that only half of the planned data centers will end up being deployed on their current schedule. Many will be significantly delayed if not outright cancelled.

When the Internet Boom turned to the Internet Bust, the NASDAQ would lose 80% of its value from its peak in March 2000 to its trough in August 2022. The S&P 500 would drop over 40%. Approximately 90% of the fiber laid during the boom would lay 'dark' for years. Some high-profile companies from the era, like Webvan, Pets.com, theGlobe.com, AskJeeves, etc., went into bankruptcy.

But here is the thing: the unprofitable companies listed above never had a combined market value of much over $10 billion at their peak. In contrast, OpenAI and Anthropic, neither of which is projected to be profitable for many, many years, if ever, have a combined market value north of $1 trillion as of their last valuation events.

And dark fiber was relatively easy and cheap to turn back 'on' when demand justified it many years down the road. How will an idle multi-billion-dollar AI data center fare in this scenario? Especially when chips are upgraded significantly by chipmakers like NVIDIA and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), regularly putting out new, improved versions of their flagship chips every two years.

And while the internet era saw its share of vendor financing, it is nothing compared to the circular financing ecosystem that has driven so much of the AI Revolution to this point. It is like comparing the 'dead ball' to the 'steroid' era in baseball.

Conclusion:

There is no doubt AI will trigger numerous changes around the economy and society in the years ahead. This technology's impact on the jobs market is increasingly uncertain and could be profoundly negative.

The purpose of this article was twofold. The first was to articulate how the backdrop in the late innings of the internet revolution in 1999 was far superior to our current environment on myriad fronts. The second is to question if the current market can hold similar valuations when these turned out to be unsustainable during the last technology paradigm shift.

Maybe things 'will be different this time.' However, history teaches us these can be five of the most dangerous words in the English language for investors.

data.title: Alphabet: AI-Powered Google Finance Is A Masterstroke
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) recently expanded its AI-powered Google Finance product to a worldwide audience. The product, which had previously been released in the U.S., was rolled out to over 100 countries, marking Google Finance’s official transition to its new AI-first era. The move was interesting in two ways. First, it showed that Google was committed to its finance product, one that some previously had thought to have been neglected; second, it showed Google’s increasing commitment to incorporating generative AI into its products.

It’s the latter aspect of Google’s new finance product that is most significant from the perspective of an investor. Generative AI has been quite possibly the biggest theme in the markets since 2023, when Silicon Valley started grabbing pieces of the pie shown to exist by the launch of ChatGPT the prior year.

In January of 2022, ChatGPT surpassed the record for fastest-growing app in history to that date, reaching 100 million users just two months after launching. The app’s success sparked a flurry of investment in generative AI, a costly enterprise that is projected to reach $700 billion in capital expenditure (CAPEX) by the end of 2026. So far, big tech companies are having little success driving profitability simply by developing AI models. The real money so far is in semiconductors, infrastructure, and potentially, in AI integrations in traditional products. The competition in these parts of the AI industry is generally less than model building, with far more opportunities for differentiation.

What is an AI integration? Put simply, it is an integration of AI into a product that isn’t inherently about AI. Examples include Meta Platforms’ (META) AI content moderation, X’s Grok chatbot, and Adobe’s (ADBE) Firefly. These integrations add a bit of AI “spice” to products that users have known and loved since long before the AI era. Developing AI in this direction may yield more profit than spending billions a year on new models, as it lends itself to more differentiation and is less competitive.

Enter Google Finance. The opportunity set here is apparently a large one, with top companies in the financial data space often earning $5 billion per year or more. Even for a company at Alphabet’s scale ($132 billion in annual profit), that’s a non-negligible opportunity. If Google Finance could be grown and scaled to the size of the biggest financial data platforms, it could potentially move the needle on the bottom line by 3% to 4% (that is, the biggest financial data companies’ annual profit divided by Google’s annual total profit).

When I last wrote about Google, I rated the stock a Buy on the grounds that the company’s massive AI spending was beginning to yield results. Since then, we have seen some of the most concrete results seen yet in the form of AI-powered Google Finance. The product has gone from a minor offering whose main use was to drive traffic to other Google products to a real research tool that provides value to investors. This is exactly the kind of AI investment that could lead to earnings growth down the line. So, I am maintaining my Google rating at Buy.

The New Google Finance - Why It’s a Big Deal

Before going any further, I should explain why the new Google Finance is a big deal. As mentioned previously, the product was not (apparently) a major priority for Google in the past. It was seen as having potential, but that was it.

Today, the product is moving from potential to execution.

The new Google Finance has several features that take it from a mere stock quote tool to a full-featured charting and research platform. The most notable of these are advanced charts, AI news overviews, and AI deep research.

You can see one of Google Finance’s new advanced charts below. It’s a candlestick chart that has a simple five-day moving average imposed over it (the yellow line). The chart format is one of four available (candle, line, area, and bar). The moving average line is one of three indicator lines available (moving average ("MA"), MA convergence-divergence, and MA envelope).

Another feature that the new Google Finance has is "deep search," a tool that provides deep financial research reports in response to user queries. These searches superficially resemble ChatGPT-like chatbot interactions, but they are more like deep research reports, pulling data from hundreds of simultaneous searches to give you the information you want. Additionally, these searches are directly plugged into Google Finance and the copious amounts of data it has on file, potentially leading to improved and/or faster results.

The power of AI-powered Google Finance had been known to American users for some time. What changed last week was the rollout of the new product to global markets. That indicated that the American beta test was successful enough to bring the product to a wider market, a sign that the product was being received well by users.

Potential Revenue and Earnings Impacts

In the next section I will show that the really bullish signal we’re getting from the new Google Finance is not so much the product itself, as the overall direction we're seeing Google moving in. Before that, though, I should get into the potential revenue and earnings impacts of the revamped app itself, because they are not nothing.

Dedale Intelligence predicts that the financial data industry will be doing $60 billion per year in revenue by 2030. It’s a highly profitable industry, with 30% net margins being quite common. If Google could capture 20% of the market and earn typical margins on its revenue in it, that would be a $4.2 billion per year earnings impact. Even for a company earning $132, that is a sizable percentage impact. So, Google could make some serious money here, just from Google Finance itself.

The Bigger Point

The bigger picture, though—and this is really where the bulk of my bullishness comes from—is that Google’s new finance app shows the company’s commitment to integrating AI into its core products. The opportunity set here is nearly unlimited. By integrating AI across its core business offerings, Google can (for example):

  • Offer Google Cloud developers tools that plug into Google’s AI ecosystem.

  • Create ads (copy and design) for Google advertisers.

  • Automate YouTube video editing.

  • And more.

While I can’t estimate the exact revenue and earnings impacts of these AI integrations, I know that they will be added to product lines that earn up to 75% margins. The AI integrations will add some amount of cost to running these businesses (AI workloads are very energy-intensive), but if they can ramp up growth rates, then they may prove to be worth it in the end. Also, the model of charging money for access to special AI tools (Google AI Pro) is proving very popular, with 150 million Google AI Pro users already recorded. The possibility of more high-end paid subscriptions is very real.

Valuation

Now let’s consider GOOG/GOOGL’s valuation multiples in light of what we have already learned.

At today’s prices, Google is an optically expensive stock, trading at 29 times earnings, 9.5 times sales, 9.2 times book, 23 times operating cash flow, and 52 times free cash flow. If you had no relative picture of how the company was likely to perform going forward, you’d probably conclude it was quite expensive.

The multiples above are cause for concern to some extent. But note all that we wrote above. By integrating AI into products like Google Finance, Google is radically upping its game, turning what were previously small features into full-fledged product suites. This has the potential to bring these products to whole new audiences or turn previous casual users into paying customers. The potential for new revenue streams and high margins is considerable. So, GOOG and GOOGL should not be valued under the assumption of negative or stagnant growth. It is quite likely the company will continue growing into the future, despite some temporary negative FCF impacts from increased CAPEX.

Risks

Despite the many exciting things that Alphabet has going for it today, the company does face risks, including:

  1. Competition from other generative AI companies. Virtually all of the Magnificent Seven tech companies are investing heavily in AI now. There is accordingly emerging fierce competition in AI model development. Potentially there could also be increased competition in apps like Google Finance as well.

  2. CAPEX costs. Google’s AI-related CAPEX is expected to hit $146 billion this year, an amount that would almost certainly weigh massively on the company’s FCF. If the company feels that it has to keep buying state-of-the-art AI GPUs every year to stay relevant, and if the GPUs remain very expensive, then the company is likely to suffer lower-than-expected long-term FCF.

Despite these risks, Alphabet/Google looks like a good opportunity today. It is growing quickly, has thick margins, and is not even particularly richly valued. On top of that, the company continues to develop world-class apps and products, as the recent Google Finance revamp showed. World-class products have always been Google’s bread and butter. As long as the company remains focused on what it does best, it will grow and compound long term.

data.title: Fastenal's Plunge Doesn't Make It Cheap Enough For An Upgrade
data.description:
April 13 was a really rough day for shares of Fastenal Company (FAST). The stock dropped around 7.3% during the day after management announced financial results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. This is peculiar because revenue came in above what analysts were anticipating, and earnings per share matched expectations. The company continues to grow nicely, though it did acknowledge some challenges facing it. To be honest with you, I am not terribly surprised that the stock declined like it did.

Don't get me wrong. It is a fantastic business with a great track record of expansion. Long term, I am bullish about it from an operational standpoint. However, as I detailed in my last article about it, published in December of last year, shares looked drastically overpriced. Despite the growth that the company is exhibiting, it is trading at levels that are difficult to justify. And that is why I called it a "Sell" back then. Admittedly, the stock has risen 9.8% since then, while the S&P 500 is down 0.8%. However, the data is clear that shares are quite pricey right now. From a valuation standpoint, it looks almost as pricey as it did back then, with the disparity driven by changes in operational performance. So I really don't see a reason to upgrade it at this time.

A solid quarter

Objectively speaking, the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was a good time for shareholders of Fastenal Company. The company reported revenue of $2.20 billion. That translated to a 12.4% increase over the $1.96 billion that the business reported a year earlier. It also happened to be $5.2 million greater than what analysts were hoping to see. This overall rise in revenue was thanks to strong customer contract signings that the company has seen since the first quarter of 2024, not to mention additional industrial production that the company benefited from in the first quarter of this year. There was a bit of a benefit caused by foreign currency fluctuations, but that accounted for just a very small portion of the overall sales increase.

Two things really stand out to me here. The first is that the company benefited to the tune of 3.5% from higher product pricing. Management mentioned that tariffs unfortunately rose faster than pricing. This helps to explain why the gross profit margin for the company dropped around 50 basis points year over year and ended up being 40 basis points lower than what management had targeted. This was because tariffs caused a delay in pricing discussions for the company and resulted in hesitation and pushback from some customers. This is problematic in and of itself, and it does point to some issues that the business might face moving forward. Management also invoked manufacturing PMI in their earnings call. In March, for instance, it was 52.7. That indicates continued expansion in the manufacturing economy, representing the 17th month in a row that we have seen expansion.

When I pulled up the PMI data for myself, I did notice in analyzing the data that's available that new orders have returned to a state of contraction, which could be a leading indicator that there could be some weakness that lies ahead. Management seems to support the view that the economy is doing decently, but far from great. If anything, I think it would be appropriate to say that things are fragile right now. This conforms with my own view of the state of the economy, which I have detailed in other articles.

The other thing that really stood out here was the fact that the company continues to grow because of its largest customers. During the quarter, revenue from those businesses that bring in $50,000 per month or more in revenue came in at $1.24 billion. That happened to be 21.2% above the $1.01 billion reported the previous year. This does not imply that we haven't seen growth elsewhere. For instance, customers that bring in $10,000 per month or more in revenue but less than $50,000 per month ended up seeing a growth rate of 6.5%. And those bringing in $5,000 per month or more but less than $10,000 ended up seeing growth of only 0.3%. While all growth is positive, the fact that more and more revenue is coming from larger customers means that there is downward pressure on profit margins as well. After all, larger customers have more negotiating power with the company than the smaller customers do. It also means that, to the extent that we can see the company’s financial performance as a barometer of the industrial economy, strength is being concentrated among the largest firms.

The business also benefited from new contract sales, as I mentioned already. Even though the number of sites per month for the firm contracted from 101,044 to 92,445, the business continues to benefit from a rise in the number of FASTBin/FASTVend signings. Year over year, the number of signings per day jumped from 102 to 110, resulting in the total number of installations expanding from 129,996 MEUs last year to 137,702 MEUs this year. Management mentioned that they continue to target between 28,000 and 30,000 device signings this year, so that's positive in and of itself.

As revenue for the company increased, profitability also increased. Earnings per share grew from $0.26 to $0.30, matching what analysts were hoping to see. This meant a rise in net profits from $298.7 million to $339.8 million. All other profitability metrics improved also, even though margins pulled back for the reasons I mentioned previously. Operating cash flow, for instance, jumped from $262.2 million to $378.4 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $347.6 million to $387.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $438.6 million to $492.2 million.

If we project out financial performance based on how the company performed in the first quarter compared to the same time last year, we would get net profits for 2026 of $1.43 billion. This compares to the $1.26 billion that the company reported a year earlier. And it can be driven primarily by the fact that management still anticipates double-digit revenue growth for the year. Other profitability metrics, scaling up the same way, would increase, with adjusted operating cash flow expanding from $1.45 billion last year to $1.61 billion this year. Similarly, EBITDA for the business should grow from $1.83 billion to $2.06 billion.

Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. Shares are, if you ask me, very expensive right now. Even on a forward basis, they are a bit difficult to justify. You also factor in my own view that the economy is going to worsen instead of improve moving forward, and I firmly believe that it is risky to bank on such an expensive company, no matter how high quality and how fast growing it is. Relative to other similar firms, the chart above also shows how the stock is priced. On a price-to-earnings basis, it is the most expensive of the group. Four of the five firms that I compared it to are cheaper than it is on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. And three of them are cheaper on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis. So even relative to other comparable firms, the company is perhaps a bit pricey.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to be bullish about Fastenal Company from an investment perspective, I just can't bring myself to do it. The company has a lot going for it. But on the whole, the stock looks too expensive for my liking, especially considering how the economy is doing. Long term, I have no doubt about the operational potential of the business. But as things stand, the best thing I can do is to call it a soft "Sell" right now.
